title: Context Engineering: Why Your Agent's Memory Architecture Is Wrong by Default
excerpt: Most agent developers treat the context window as an append-only database and reach for a vector store when the agent starts forgetting things. The correct mental model is RAM: fast, finite working memory that needs deliberate tiers below it — and the write problem is harder than the read problem.
content:
## TL;DR

- The context window is working memory, not storage. Every token you put in it competes with every other token. Appending indefinitely degrades performance before you hit the hard ceiling.
- There are three tiers that survive production: in-context working memory, external retrieval (warm), and persistent structured state (cold). Each has a different read/write contract.
- Deciding *what to write* to persistent memory is significantly harder than deciding how to read from it. Most frameworks paper over this with naive summarization.
- Letta's git-backed context repositories are an interesting solution to a specific hard problem: concurrent writes from multiple subagents. Worth understanding even if you're not using Letta.

## Why this matters

The "agent forgot what I told it three turns ago" problem is real, and the instinct to solve it by adding a vector database is understandable but often wrong. A vector store solves the *retrieval* problem. It does nothing about the harder question of whether the agent wrote anything worth retrieving in the first place. Engineers who have shipped multi-session agents will recognize the pattern: elaborate retrieval infrastructure on top of shallow memory writes, with the system confidently surfacing the wrong facts at query time. Before reaching for more infrastructure, it's worth getting the mental model right.

## What's actually going on

### The RAM metaphor, and why it matters

The context window is bounded working memory. Tokens at different positions have different effective weights — very long contexts degrade recall on items in the middle [3], and every irrelevant token you include is competing with the tokens that actually matter. Treating the context window as a database that you append to indefinitely works fine in demos and falls apart in production.

The right analogy is RAM in a program with a fixed heap. You have fast access to everything currently in it, but you cannot fit the whole application state there at once, writing to it has side effects on everything that reads it, and you need a deliberate architecture for what gets paged out and when.

### The three tiers

**Tier 1: In-context working memory.** This is everything currently in the prompt — the system prompt, conversation history, injected structured state, and any retrieved snippets. It's the only memory the model actually *sees*. It is fast and always relevant to the current inference, but it is finite and ephemeral. When the session ends, it's gone unless something explicitly writes it down.

**Tier 2: External retrieval (warm memory).** Vector stores, keyword indexes, embedding search — all of this lives here. You write facts to it and read them back via semantic or keyword query. The read latency is low enough to inject results into context before inference. The problem is that retrieval is a probabilistic operation: the AMA-Bench evaluation found that "similarity-based retrieval is unreliable," with HippoRAG2 dropping 43.2% accuracy when moving from constructed memory (where the correct needle is guaranteed present) to end-to-end retrieval [3]. Retrieval infrastructure is not a memory solution; it is a recall mechanism on top of a memory solution.

**Tier 3: Persistent structured state (cold memory).** This is the agent's long-term knowledge — a typed schema of what it knows about a user, task, or domain. The OpenAI context personalization cookbook [4] implements this as a structured profile (typed fields like seat_preference, loyalty_status) plus a set of timestamped notes. Reads are explicit: you render the state into the system prompt at session start. Writes are deliberate: a consolidation step at the end of each session decides what gets promoted from session memory to global memory, what gets deduplicated, and what gets dropped. The cookbook's consolidation prompt includes an explicit instruction: "Do NOT invent new facts. Only use what appears in the input notes" [4] — a guard against the model hallucinating memories during summarization.

### The write problem is harder

Most of the tooling and literature focuses on retrieval — embedding models, vector store backends, rerankers. The write side gets much less attention, but it is where most production memory failures originate.

The questions on the write side:
- When do you write? (After every turn? At session end? When confidence exceeds a threshold?)
- What do you write? (The raw exchange? A model-generated summary? Extracted structured facts?)
- How do you handle contradiction? (A new note that conflicts with an existing one needs resolution logic, not just appending.)
- How do you handle forgetting? (Notes that are only relevant to one session should be dropped, not promoted to global memory.)

The OpenAI cookbook's session-end consolidation addresses all four explicitly: it deduplicates semantically equivalent notes, resolves conflicts by keeping the most recent, and explicitly drops ephemeral session-only observations [4]. This is not exotic — it is careful bookkeeping. The point is that someone has to implement this logic, and "dump it all in the vector store" is not an answer.

AMA-Bench found that "varying the memory architecture induces significantly higher variance" than model size, with score ranges reaching 0.45 across memory system variants [3]. You will get more leverage from better write logic than from a bigger model.

### Filesystem-as-memory

One finding that surprises people: storing agent memory as plain files on a filesystem performs competitively on long-horizon benchmarks. Mem0's 2026 State of AI Agent Memory report [2] includes LoCoMo and LongMemEval benchmark comparisons, though I want to be direct about the source: this is a vendor-published report from a company that builds memory infrastructure. Their own algorithm scores 91.6 on LoCoMo. I could not verify a specific "filesystem at ~74%" figure I had seen attributed to this report — the fetched content did not surface it. Take any comparison numbers from this source as a starting point for your own evaluation, not as ground truth.

The intuition behind why filesystem-based memory is competitive is sound regardless: plain text files give the agent (or a retrieval system) structured navigation, token-efficient frontmatter for quick scanning, and a human-readable representation that is easy to inspect and debug. Letta's approach [1] makes this explicit: their context repositories use a file tree where the directory hierarchy and file names act as navigational signals, and a system/ directory designates which files are always fully loaded into the system prompt. You do not need semantic embedding search to find a file called user/preferences.md.

### Letta's git-backed context repositories

Letta's context repository design [1] is interesting for a specific reason that has nothing to do with whether you use Letta: it addresses concurrent writes from multiple subagents, which is a genuinely hard coordination problem.

In a single-agent, single-session setup, memory writes are sequential and the ordering is clear. In a multi-agent system where subagents are running in parallel, you have the standard distributed systems problem of concurrent writes to shared state. Two subagents processing different parts of a task simultaneously both want to update the shared memory. Without coordination, you get last-write-wins corruption or conflicts that silently discard one agent's output.

Letta's solution: give each subagent an isolated git worktree. Each subagent writes to its own working copy of the memory repository. When they finish, their changes merge back through git's conflict resolution. Every change is versioned with a commit message [1].

What the git metaphor buys you:
- **Isolation**: subagents can't corrupt each other's in-progress writes
- **Auditability**: you have a full history of every memory change, who made it, and when
- **Rollback**: if a subagent writes bad information, you can revert to a known good state
- **Conflict visibility**: when two subagents contradict each other, git surfaces the conflict explicitly rather than silently dropping one

This is not the right architecture for every use case — a single-agent chatbot does not need worktree isolation. But if you are building multi-agent systems where subagents run concurrently and share memory state, this pattern solves a real problem that most frameworks either ignore or handle with coarse-grained locking.

## When do you actually need a vector store

A vector store makes sense when:
- Your memory corpus is large enough that loading it all into context is impractical (thousands of notes, not dozens)
- The relevant memories are genuinely unpredictable from session to session, so you cannot pre-select what to inject
- You have invested in write logic that produces retrieval-worthy chunks (not raw conversation transcripts)

A vector store is cargo cult when:
- You have fewer than a few hundred memory entries — a simple prefix scan or keyword index is faster, cheaper, and more debuggable
- Your retrieval is hitting the same notes every session — those should be in the structured profile, not in a retrieval corpus
- You haven't defined your write logic yet — adding semantic search on top of unstructured notes does not fix the fundamental problem

The AMA-Bench results reinforce this: "the long context baseline is consistently strong and often achieves the best performance, whereas existing memory systems exhibit large variance" [3]. For many tasks, carefully curating what goes into context beats a retrieval layer over everything. The baseline is harder to beat than the tooling ecosystem implies.

## Caveats and open questions

**The benchmark landscape is messy.** LoCoMo, LongMemEval, AMA-Bench, BEAM — each measures different things with different methodologies. The Mem0 report [2] is vendor-sourced and compares against their own prior algorithm, not against a neutral independent baseline. AMA-Bench [3] is academic and more credible, but it covers agentic trajectories that may not match your use case. No single benchmark tells you how memory will perform on your workload.

**The write logic problem is unsolved at the ecosystem level.** Most frameworks (LangGraph, LangChain, CrewAI) give you retrieval infrastructure and leave write logic to you. Some defaults (summarize the last N turns) are actively harmful for long-horizon tasks where subtle facts get dropped. This is an area where you should have opinions rather than accepting framework defaults.

**Git-backed memory has real operational costs.** Worktrees, merge conflicts, and git operations add latency and complexity. Letta's approach [1] makes sense when concurrent writes are a genuine bottleneck. Adopting it when you have one agent with sequential memory writes is over-engineering.

**Memory and privacy are in tension.** The OpenAI cookbook [4] explicitly bans storing "passport numbers, payment details, SSNs, full DOB, addresses" in the memory notes. This is table stakes, not a complete answer. Long-lived memory stores that accumulate facts about users over months become a significant privacy surface — one that most memory library documentation addresses superficially if at all.

## References

1. [Letta: Context Repositories — git-backed memory for agents](https://www.letta.com/blog/context-repositories)
2. [Mem0: State of AI Agent Memory 2026](https://mem0.ai/blog/state-of-ai-agent-memory-2026)
3. [AMA-Bench: Long-Horizon Memory Evaluation (arXiv 2602.22769)](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.22769v1)
4. [OpenAI Cookbook: Context Engineering for Personalization](https://developers.openai.com/cookbook/examples/agents_sdk/context_personalization)
author: Samer Alkhodary

sources.title: Mem0: State of AI Agent Memory 2026
OpenAI Cookbook: Context Engineering for Personalization

title: Multi-Agent vs Single-Agent: Concrete Engineering Tradeoffs
excerpt: The instinct to decompose a struggling agent into a multi-agent system is common and often premature. Here are the actual token costs, failure modes, and architectural tradeoffs across single-agent, supervisor, handoff, and swarm patterns — grounded in published data, not framework marketing.
content:
## TL;DR

- A single agent with many tools is the right default. Multi-agent is a scaling tool, not an architecture upgrade.
- Multi-agent systems use roughly 15x more tokens than standard chat interactions; single-agent uses roughly 4x. That cost spread is real and compounds fast at scale [1].
- Parallelism is the clearest win for multi-agent: Anthropic cut research time by up to 90% on complex queries by running subagents in parallel [1]. But parallel agents share state poorly and produce conflicting outputs when not designed carefully [2].
- Before going multi-agent: if your task fits one agent with 3-10 tool calls, it probably should [1].

## Why this matters

The default instinct when an agent starts underperforming is to decompose it — spin up a supervisor, hand work off to specialists, run things in parallel. Sometimes that is the right call. But multi-agent systems introduce coordination overhead, token costs, and failure modes that single-agent systems sidestep entirely. If you are building your next agent and choosing an architecture, the decision deserves a concrete engineering analysis, not a pattern selection from a framework README.

## The patterns and what they actually cost

### Single agent + tools

One LLM, one context window, a set of registered tools. The agent decides when to call which tool, synthesizes results, and responds. This is the baseline.

What you get: full shared context at all times, deterministic call chains if you want them, easy tracing, and the lowest token overhead of any approach. Debugging means reading one log. State sharing is trivial — it is all in one context.

What breaks it: too many unrelated instructions or tools crammed into a single context. Arruda's Shopify team observed this directly — monolithic prompts where "you have too many unrelated tokens, too many instructions, the LLM gets lost, and the result was very poor" [6]. At that point, decomposition is not premature optimization; it is necessary.

Anthropic's internal decision rule is concrete: simple fact-finding maps to one agent with 3-10 tool calls [1]. If your task fits that description, the added complexity of any multi-agent pattern is overhead you are paying for nothing.

### Supervisor / manager pattern

A central LLM owns the conversation and delegates to specialists, either via Agent.as_tool() (OpenAI Agents SDK) or tool-call-based routing (LangGraph). The supervisor sees all inputs and outputs; subagents do bounded subtasks and return results.

This is the most common multi-agent pattern and has a real use case: parallel execution of independent subtasks. Anthropic's research system runs lead agents that spawn 3-5 subagents in parallel, each using 3+ tools concurrently, cutting research time by up to 90% for complex queries [1]. Shopify decomposed a theme review process that took 22 hours as a monolith down to 7-20 minutes by assigning each review criterion to a separate specialized agent [6].

The token cost for a multi-agent system sits at roughly 15x the baseline chat interaction [1]. A single agent runs roughly 4x baseline. That 3-4x gap between single-agent and multi-agent is the cost of coordination, repeated context injection, and subagent scaffolding — before you account for supervisor round trips.

One concrete efficiency lever: Lin et al. found that a SupervisorAgent framework reduced token consumption in the Smolagents framework by 29.68% on the GAIA benchmark without compromising success rates, validated across five additional benchmarks [5]. The gain comes from runtime supervision that filters redundant observations and corrects errors before they propagate. Naive supervisor implementations waste tokens, and there is real room to recover some of that cost with smarter orchestration.

One note on tooling: LangChain's own README for the langgraph-supervisor library now reads: "We now recommend using the supervisor pattern directly via tools rather than this library for most use cases. The tool-calling approach gives you more control over context engineering." [4] The library's own maintainers walking back the abstraction in favor of more explicit tool calls is worth paying attention to.

### Handoff / swarm pattern

A triage agent routes the conversation to a specialist, which becomes the active agent. Unlike agents-as-tools, control transfers — the specialist is not called and returned from; it takes over the remainder of the turn.

In the OpenAI Agents SDK, the receiving agent sees the full prior conversation history by default. You can filter this via input filters or the (beta) nested handoff history option, which collapses prior context into a summarized message [3]. The tradeoff is direct: full history means the specialist has complete context but pays for it in tokens on every call; filtered history is cheaper but risks losing relevant detail.

The handoffs documentation flags a guardrail gap worth knowing: "Handoffs stay within a single run. Input guardrails still apply only to the first agent in the chain, and output guardrails only to the agent that produces the final output." [3] If your safety or compliance setup assumes guardrail coverage at every step, a handoff chain breaks that assumption silently.

The SDK docs also make a point that applies across all these patterns: code-based orchestration, where routing logic lives in your code rather than in an LLM, "makes tasks more deterministic and predictable, in terms of speed, cost and performance" [3]. For triage workflows where routing rules are finite and known, hard-coded routing beats an LLM deciding where to hand off — it is cheaper, faster, and you can test it.

### Hierarchical and nested patterns

Supervisors of supervisors. Token cost compounds at every level, and debugging a failure requires tracing the full call tree. None of the sources I have give separately benchmarked cost figures for hierarchical vs. flat multi-agent topologies — I am not going to invent a comparison that does not exist in the data. What the sources do show: Anthropic's research system uses explicit effort ceilings (simple = 1 agent, complex = 10+ subagents) rather than treating deeper hierarchy as the default [1]. The pattern is real, but the recommendation from practitioners is to reach for it only when task complexity genuinely demands it.

## The parallel execution problem

The strongest argument for multi-agent is latency reduction, not quality improvement. Token-for-token, a multi-agent system running subagents in parallel can finish faster than a single agent working sequentially, even though it costs more. Anthropic's data is specific: parallel execution cut research time by up to 90% for complex queries, and token usage explains 80% of performance variance on BrowseComp [1]. Running subagents in parallel is how you scale token budget without linearly scaling wall time.

But parallel subagents share state poorly. Yan describes the failure mode clearly: two parallel subagents building a Flappy Bird clone — one produces a Super Mario Bros background, the other an incompatible bird asset, and the downstream agent cannot reconcile them [2]. This is not a contrived edge case; it is the natural outcome of agents that make implicit decisions without shared context. Yan's principle applies directly: "Actions carry implicit decisions, and conflicting decisions carry bad results." [2]

Claude Code's architectural response to this is deliberate: it never runs work in parallel with a subtask agent, and subtask agents are typically only tasked with answering questions, not writing code [2]. Sequential is slower but sidesteps state conflict entirely.

## Caveats and open questions

**No clean per-pattern cost breakdown exists in the public literature.** The 15x vs. 4x token figures from Anthropic [1] are high-level — multi-agent vs. single-agent. There is no published, source-backed number comparing supervisor vs. swarm vs. hierarchical token overhead. If that breakdown matters to your decision, benchmark your own workload.

**Latency numbers by pattern are not in the sources.** Anthropic's 90% research time reduction [1] is specific to parallel execution in their research system. Shopify's 22-hours-to-minutes improvement [6] compares a broken monolithic prompt against a well-designed multi-agent system — not a clean A/B of architectures. Both are real, neither generalizes cleanly to your workload.

**Debugging is qualitatively harder, not quantified.** Arruda's mitigation was running agents in-process via Ruby fibers rather than over the network, making mid-event tracking straightforward [6]. That is a useful pattern — keeping agents in-process during development preserves observability. But the sources do not quantify the debugging overhead as you add agents. It gets worse; by how much depends on your tooling.

**Benchmark provenance matters.** Anthropic's 90.2% multi-agent improvement is on Anthropic's internal research evaluation [1]. Lin et al.'s 29.68% token reduction is on GAIA [5]. These numbers are real, but they describe specific task distributions. Treat them as directional signals, not guarantees.

**Guardrail coverage gaps in handoff chains.** If your safety requirements assume input and output guardrails at every step, the OpenAI Agents SDK handoff architecture requires explicit re-examination — guardrails only fire at the first and last agents in the chain [3].

## References

1. [Anthropic — How we built our multi-agent research system](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/multi-agent-research-system)
2. [Cognition — Don't Build Multi-Agents (Walden Yan)](https://cognition.ai/blog/dont-build-multi-agents)
3. [OpenAI Agents SDK — Agent orchestration and handoffs](https://openai.github.io/openai-agents-python/multi_agent/)
4. [LangChain — LangGraph Supervisor library (GitHub README)](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langgraph-supervisor-py)
5. [Lin et al. — Stop Wasting Your Tokens: Towards Efficient Runtime Multi-Agent Systems (arXiv 2510.26585)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.26585)
6. [Paulo Arruda (Shopify) — What I Learned Building Multi-Agent Systems From Scratch, QCon AI 2026](https://www.infoq.com/presentations/multi-agent-system-lessons/)
author: Samer Alkhodary

sources.title: Anthropic — How we built our multi-agent research system
OpenAI Agents SDK — Agent orchestration and handoffs
Lin et al. — Stop Wasting Your Tokens: Towards Efficient Runtime Multi-Agent Systems (arXiv 2510.26585)
Paulo Arruda (Shopify) — What I Learned Building Multi-Agent Systems From Scratch, QCon AI 2026

title: MCP vs Skills: Two Different Layers for Extending Agent Capabilities
excerpt: MCP and Anthropic's Agent Skills both extend what an agent can do, but they operate at different layers and solve different problems. This post breaks down the technical architecture of each, where they overlap, where they are complementary, and what signals exist about their respective trajectories.
content:
## TL;DR

- MCP is a wire protocol: it standardizes how any client connects to external tools, data sources, and services. Think of it as the integration layer.
- Skills are context-loaded capability packages: a `SKILL.md` plus optional scripts and docs that teach an agent *how to do something*, loaded progressively into the context window to manage token cost.
- They are not competing alternatives. Anthropic's own framing is explicit: MCP = connectivity, Skills = expertise. A single skill can orchestrate multiple MCP servers [4].
- MCP is now a vendor-neutral standard under the Linux Foundation's Agentic AI Foundation. Skills remain an Anthropic-specific mechanism. These are on different governance and portability trajectories.

## Why this matters

If you are building agent systems today, you have to choose what to invest in. Do you build MCP servers to expose your internal tools? Do you package reusable workflows as Skills? Both? The answer depends on understanding what each one actually is — not the marketing framing, but the technical layer it operates at and the problem it is designed to solve.

## What MCP actually is

MCP (Model Context Protocol) is an open-source client-server protocol for connecting AI applications to external tools, data, and services [1]. It defines three primitives:

- **Tools**: executable functions the model can invoke (a database query, a file write, an API call)
- **Resources**: data the server exposes for the model to read (files, records, live content)
- **Prompts**: reusable prompt templates the server can provide

An MCP server is a process that implements this protocol and exposes some set of capabilities. The client — which could be Claude Code, VS Code Copilot, Cursor, or anything else that speaks MCP — connects to those servers and makes their capabilities available to the model [1].

The protocol's architecture is small: clients maintain one-to-one connections with servers, and the host (the application, e.g., Claude Code) manages those connections and decides what gets exposed to the model [1]. In Claude Code specifically, you configure MCP servers with `claude mcp add`, and from there the model can call into Jira, Sentry, PostgreSQL, or whatever you have wired up [8].

MCP's scope is broad: it handles the connection, the capability negotiation, and the invocation protocol, but it says nothing about *how* to accomplish complex multi-step workflows. It tells the model what tools exist; it does not teach the model expert-level usage patterns for those tools.

## What Skills actually are

A Skill is a directory. Specifically:

```
.claude/skills/
  comparable-company-analysis/
    SKILL.md          # required: YAML frontmatter + instructions
    templates/        # optional extra files
    scripts/          # optional runnable code
```

The `SKILL.md` has a YAML frontmatter block with a `name` and `description` field. That metadata — roughly 100 tokens — is what the model loads at startup. When a user request matches a skill's domain, the model loads the full `SKILL.md` (under 5k tokens). If that `SKILL.md` references additional files, those are loaded on-demand via bash [3].

This three-level progressive disclosure (L1: metadata always loaded; L2: full SKILL.md on trigger; L3: referenced files on-demand) is the key design decision. The token cost at idle is negligible; the full context is only paid when the skill is actually relevant [3].

Skills live at `~/.claude/skills/` (personal, available across projects) or `.claude/skills/` (project-local) [3]. The pre-built skills from Anthropic cover document formats: PowerPoint generation, Excel manipulation, Word and PDF handling [3].

The core proposition is different from MCP's: a Skill packages *workflow expertise*, not connectivity. Anthropic frames Skills as teaching agents "more complex workflows that involve external tools and software," specifically as a way to complement MCP [2].

## Where they differ, concretely

**Protocol vs. context injection.** MCP requires a running server process that speaks the protocol. The client connects at runtime, discovers capabilities, and the model can invoke them as tool calls. A Skill is Markdown read from the filesystem. No server, no protocol negotiation, no network traffic.

**Token cost.** Willison cited GitHub's official MCP server as consuming tens of thousands of tokens when its full capability set is loaded into context [5]. A Skill's startup cost is a few dozen tokens — the L1 metadata — scaling to thousands only when actively triggered [5]. This is a meaningful difference if you are managing context budgets across many capabilities.

**Portability.** MCP is a vendor-neutral standard. Any client that implements the protocol can connect to any MCP server [1]. Skills are currently an Anthropic mechanism — `SKILL.md` in `.claude/skills/` is a Claude Code convention. Willison noted in October 2025 that Skills could work with Codex CLI and Gemini CLI; I could not independently verify cross-vendor Skills adoption from the sources in this article, so treat that as Willison's claim at that time, not an established fact [5].

**Scope of capability.** MCP is good at: "expose this tool so the model can call it." Skills are good at: "teach the model the correct multi-step workflow for this domain." These are genuinely different problems. A Skills-based Comparable Company Analysis workflow can coordinate three MCP-connected financial data sources and know the correct sequencing, fallback logic, and output format — none of which the MCP servers themselves encode [4].

## How they fit together

Anthropic's framing from December 2025 is the clearest statement of the intended relationship: MCP provides connectivity, Skills provide expertise [4]. A single skill can orchestrate multiple MCP servers. A single MCP server can support dozens of different skills [4]. They are complementary layers, not competing mechanisms.

In Claude Code, both appear as listed extension mechanisms: "Extend Claude Code with skills, connect to external services with MCP" [8]. Claude Code also documents an `alwaysLoad` flag for MCP servers (requires Claude Code v2.1.121+) that forces server tools into context at session start [8].

A practical heuristic: if you are wiring up a new data source or service so that the model can call into it, that is an MCP server. If you are encoding a reusable workflow pattern or domain expertise that the model should execute when it encounters a particular class of task, that is a Skill.

## Where each is heading (inference, not prediction)

On the MCP side: the December 2025 donation to the Agentic AI Foundation — a directed fund under the Linux Foundation, co-founded by Anthropic, Block, and OpenAI, with Google, Microsoft, AWS, Cloudflare, and Bloomberg as Platinum members — is a strong signal about trajectory [6][7]. The AAIF governs strategy and budget; technical governance remains via the Steering and Evolution Process [7]. When a protocol gets that governance structure, it is being positioned as infrastructure. The implication, as inference: MCP is being positioned as vendor-neutral infrastructure — not something any one vendor owns.

On the Skills side: there is no published roadmap. What is visible from current sources is that Skills are Claude Code-specific, actively developed, and positioned as the mechanism for encoding complex agent workflows. If MCP continues to grow as the integration standard, Skills could evolve as the layer *above* MCP — the expertise layer that agents load to know how to use what MCP exposes. But that is a reading of the architecture and current positioning, not a statement about Anthropic's plans.

Cross-vendor Skills adoption remains an open question. The Willison claim about Codex CLI and Gemini CLI compatibility would, if true, make Skills a more portable mechanism than they currently appear — but this article cannot verify it from the available sources.

## Caveats and open questions

**No head-to-head production benchmarks exist in these sources.** The Willison comparison — tens of thousands of tokens for a large MCP server vs. a few dozen for a Skill's startup metadata — is the only concrete comparative number available [5]. It is directionally meaningful but not a controlled study. Your actual token costs depend on how many MCP servers you run, what their capability sets look like, and how many Skills are idle vs. triggered at any given time.

**Skills portability is unverified.** The claim that Skills work with non-Claude clients is from a single October 2025 blog post [5]. If cross-vendor adoption is relevant to your evaluation, verify against current documentation for each client.

**Future direction is inference.** Neither Anthropic nor the MCP project has published a roadmap describing how these two mechanisms will evolve relative to each other. Everything in the "where each is heading" section is interpretation of observable signals: the AAIF governance structure, the complementary positioning language, the separate development tracks in Claude Code. Treat it as a working hypothesis, not a product announcement.

## References

1. [MCP Official Introduction](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/introduction)
2. [Anthropic Engineering — Equipping agents for the real world with Agent Skills](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/equipping-agents-for-the-real-world-with-agent-skills)
3. [Claude Platform Docs — Agent Skills Overview](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/agent-skills/overview)
4. [Anthropic — Extending Claude's capabilities with skills and MCP servers](https://claude.com/blog/extending-claude-capabilities-with-skills-mcp-servers)
5. [Simon Willison — Claude Skills are awesome, maybe a bigger deal than MCP](https://simonwillison.net/2025/Oct/16/claude-skills/)
6. [Anthropic — Donating the Model Context Protocol and establishing the Agentic AI Foundation](https://www.anthropic.com/news/donating-the-model-context-protocol-and-establishing-of-the-agentic-ai-foundation)
7. [MCP Blog — MCP joins the Agentic AI Foundation](https://blog.modelcontextprotocol.io/posts/2025-12-09-mcp-joins-agentic-ai-foundation/)
8. [Claude Code Docs — MCP](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/mcp)
author: Samer Alkhodary

sources.title: Anthropic Engineering — Equipping agents for the real world with Agent Skills
Claude Platform Docs — Agent Skills Overview
Anthropic — Extending Claude's capabilities with skills and MCP servers
Simon Willison — Claude Skills are awesome, maybe a bigger deal than MCP
Anthropic — Donating the Model Context Protocol and establishing the Agentic AI Foundation
MCP Blog — MCP joins the Agentic AI Foundation

title: From Jira Ticket to Deploy: A Working Claude Code Pipeline
excerpt: Vendor demos show Claude writing code in isolation. This article wires the full loop — Jira intake, spec, implementation, tests, PR, security review, deploy — using documented Claude Code primitives (subagents, hooks, MCP servers, claude-code-action@v1). The architectural insight is where the human gates go and why they matter, not the individual pieces.
content:
## TL;DR

- Three things converged in 2026 that make a Jira-to-deploy loop tractable without custom orchestration: the Atlassian MCP server went GA with OAuth 2.1 [2], `claude-code-action@v1` reached stable [10], and Claude Code's subagent model gives you isolated context windows per stage by default [3].
- The right shape is seven sequential subagents — not one sprawling session, and not an agent team. Each subagent has a narrow scope and a structured hand-off to the next.
- Hooks, not prompts, are the gate mechanism. `SubagentStop` blocks the pipeline until a human approves the plan. `PreToolUse` blocks the deploy command until a human confirms. Asking Claude to ask permission is not a gate.
- Security is a stage, not an afterthought. `/security-review` is a built-in Skill that runs cheap [6][7]. `/ultrareview` is the deep multi-agent pass at $5–$20 per run after three free [8].

## Why this matters

The demo circuit shows Claude writing a feature in a clean repo. The seams of a real pipeline — ticket intake, test execution, PR review, deploy approval, security audit — are where engineering judgment actually lives. If you are evaluating whether to build production infrastructure on Claude Code, the seams matter more than the happy path. This post wires the full loop using documented primitives, with explicit notes on what is stable versus experimental and where the architecture has to compose rather than integrate.

## What actually changed in 2026

Two integrations that previously carried real risk are now stable.

**Atlassian MCP server (GA February 4, 2026).** Before GA you wrote a custom Jira REST wrapper with Personal Access Token auth, managed token refresh, and pinned an API version. The hosted MCP server now handles OAuth 2.1 out of the box and supports thirteen clients including Claude Code [2]. There is no local subprocess to manage — you connect to a remote endpoint:

```bash
claude mcp add --transport http atlassian https://mcp.atlassian.com/v1/mcp
```

The remote-only model is an operational change worth understanding [1]. No npm package to version-pin, no local process supervisor, but you do have to handle OAuth scope configuration on the Atlassian side.

**`claude-code-action@v1` (GA).** Before stable, the GitHub Actions integration was a beta with documented breaking-change risk. @v1 is now stable [10]. The claude_args parameter accepts real flags including --max-turns, --model, --mcp-config, --allowedTools, --debug, and --append-system-prompt [10]. Wiring this into a production CI pipeline is now a reasonable call.

**Subagents have stable semantics.** A subagent runs in its own context window with its own system prompt and its own tool allowlist [3]. There is no frontmatter field to enable this — isolation is the default. That property is what makes a multi-stage pipeline tractable: the implementer subagent does not carry the ticket-intake conversation history into its decisions.

## The seven-stage pipeline

```text
Jira ticket
    │
    ▼
[Stage 1] ticket-intake subagent
    │
    ▼
[Stage 2] spec-writer subagent
    │   ◀── HUMAN GATE: plan approval (SubagentStop hook, exit 2)
    ▼
[Stage 3] implementer subagent
    │
    ▼
[Stage 4] test-writer subagent (separate context)
    │
    ▼
[Stage 5] pr-creator subagent
    │   ◀── QUALITY GATE: branch protection (CI must pass)
    ▼
[Stage 6] security-reviewer subagent
    │   /security-review skill + optional /ultrareview
    ▼
[Stage 7] deploy command
        ◀── HUMAN GATE: PreToolUse hook (jq + read confirmation)
```

All seven stages are subagents. The parent session is the orchestrator: it spawns each subagent in sequence, passes structured JSON between them, and decides whether to proceed.

A single large Skill could describe these steps, but subagents make the pipeline operationally safer. Each stage gets its own context, permissions, and handoff boundary, which reduces context pollution, limits tool access, and makes human approval between stages enforceable.

## Stage 1 — Ticket intake

The subagent calls `mcp__atlassian__issue_read` (the `mcp__<server>__<tool>` namespace pattern is documented in the hooks reference, where matchers like `mcp__memory__.*` are shown [5]). It returns structured JSON — not prose — that downstream stages consume.

```yaml
# .claude/agents/ticket-intake.md
---
name: ticket-intake
description: Read a Jira ticket and return a structured spec object
tools:
  - mcp__atlassian__issue_read
  - mcp__atlassian__search_issues
---
Read the Jira ticket at $TICKET_KEY. Pull any linked issues.
Return JSON with: title, acceptance_criteria (array), linked_tickets,
labels, and known_constraints. Do not write code or modify files.
```

Scope this subagent to read-only. The Atlassian MCP server supports OAuth 2.1, but the scope of the token still has to be configured on the Atlassian side — do that explicitly rather than handing over a wide-permission token [1].

## Stage 2 — Spec generation

The spec-writer reads the JSON from Stage 1 and produces an implementation spec: which files change, the approach, the edge cases, and the open questions. It does not write code.

```yaml
# .claude/agents/spec-writer.md
---
name: spec-writer
description: Generate an implementation spec from ticket data
tools:
  - Read
  - Bash
---
Given the ticket JSON from ticket-intake, produce a technical spec.
List affected files (with paths), implementation approach (2-3 paragraphs),
edge cases, and open questions. Use Read to inspect actual files before
listing them. Do not modify anything.
```

## Human gate — Plan approval

This is the most important gate in the pipeline. Catching a misinterpreted acceptance criterion before any code is written costs a 30-second review. Catching it after costs a revert and another CI run. The right mechanism is a `SubagentStop` hook that exits with code 2 to block the pipeline [5]:

```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "SubagentStop": [
      {
        "command": "bash -c 'echo Plan ready. APPROVE/REJECT? && read -r ans </dev/tty && [ \"$ans\" = APPROVE ] || exit 2'"
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

`SubagentStop` fires whenever any subagent finishes [5]. If you want per-subagent gating — approve the spec but auto-pass test generation — you need conditional logic in the hook command keyed off `$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR` state, or invoke distinct named subagents you can branch on. The current hook model does not support per-subagent name matching directly.

Exit code 2 from a hook blocks Claude from proceeding [5]. Exit 0 lets it continue. The operator sees the spec, types APPROVE or REJECT, and the pipeline halts or continues.

## Stage 3 — Implementation

The implementer subagent receives the approved spec and writes the code. Scope is strictly bounded to the files listed in the spec.

```yaml
# .claude/agents/implementer.md
---
name: implementer
description: Implement changes per the approved spec
tools:
  - Read
  - Edit
  - Write
  - Bash
---
Implement the spec. Modify only the files listed. Do not refactor outside
the specified scope. Run the existing test suite after implementation
and report results.
```

## Stage 4 — Test generation

A separate subagent in its own context. Keeping test generation isolated from implementation avoids the failure mode where the same model that wrote the code also writes tests encoding the same assumptions — a common source of tests that pass while the behavior is wrong.

## Stage 5 — PR creation

The GitHub MCP server exposes:
- create_pull_request
- actions_list
- get_job_logs
- issue_read
- issue_write [9]

The pr-creator subagent commits the changes, opens a draft PR, and polls Actions status until CI completes:

```yaml
# .claude/agents/pr-creator.md
---
name: pr-creator
description: Open a PR and verify CI passes
tools:
  - Bash
  - mcp__github__create_pull_request
  - mcp__github__actions_list
  - mcp__github__get_job_logs
---
Commit changes on a feature branch. Open a draft PR with title from the
ticket, body listing acceptance criteria as a checklist, and a link back
to the Jira ticket. Poll actions_list until CI completes. If failures,
fetch logs via get_job_logs and surface them.
```

Merge approval is enforced by GitHub branch protection rules — not by Claude. This is deliberate: quality gates (CI must pass) and human-approval gates (human must click merge) are architecturally distinct. Branch protection enforces both deterministically. Claude does not need to ask whether it has permission to merge.

For the CI side, `claude-code-action@v1` runs inside a workflow triggered on PR open:

```yaml
# .github/workflows/claude-review.yml
name: Claude PR Review
on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize]
jobs:
  claude-review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        with:
          claude_code_oauth_token: ${{ secrets.CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN }}
          claude_args: "--max-turns 10"
```

`--max-turns 10` is a real, documented flag that bounds the agent loop — appropriate for a CI review job where you want predictable, finite behavior [10].

## Stage 6 — Security assessment

`/security-review` is a built-in command discoverable via the Skill tool, confirmed in the Week 16 changelog covering versions v2.1.105–v2.1.113 (April 13–17, 2026) [7] and documented in the Skills reference [6]. You invoke it from inside a security subagent:

```yaml
# .claude/agents/security-reviewer.md
---
name: security-reviewer
description: Run a security review on the changes in the current branch
---
Invoke the /security-review skill on the changes introduced by the
implementer in this branch. Focus on: authentication, input validation,
secrets handling, dependency vulnerabilities. Output a structured finding
list with severity, location, and recommended fix.
```

For a deeper pass, `/ultrareview` (research preview, requires v2.1.86+) launches a multi-agent fleet that runs in a remote sandbox and independently verifies each finding before reporting [8]. The verification step matters — single-pass model output produces false positives that erode trust in the security stage. Cost is documented: three free runs, then $5–$20 per run, with a 5–10 minute runtime [8]. Whether that cost is worth it depends on the change — overkill for a CSS refactor, appropriate before shipping an authentication change.

The security stage runs after the PR exists but before deploy. Findings can be filed as comments on the PR or as new Jira tickets via the Atlassian MCP server, closing the loop.

## Stage 7 — Deploy with a PreToolUse gate

There is no single primary source documenting an integrated Jira-to-deploy Claude Code reference architecture. The deploy step is a composition pattern: your existing deploy CLI is gated by a `PreToolUse` hook on `Bash` [5].

Common deploy commands include:
- `vercel deploy --prebuilt`
- `aws cloudformation deploy`
- `fly deploy`

`PreToolUse` is the only hook type that can block a tool call before it executes [5].

```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "PreToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Bash",
        "command": "bash -c 'cmd=$(jq -r .tool_input.command); if echo \"$cmd\" | grep -qE \"(deploy|release|push.*prod)\"; then echo \"DEPLOY DETECTED: $cmd\" >&2 && read -p \"Confirm? [yes/no]: \" ans </dev/tty && [ \"$ans\" = yes ] || exit 2; fi'"
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

The hook reads the tool input as JSON from stdin via `jq` (the documented input mechanism for command hooks [5]), extracts the Bash command, pattern-matches against deploy verbs, and surfaces the exact command to the operator. Exit code 2 cancels the tool call without terminating the session. Any non-deploy Bash call passes through unchanged.

## Where the human gates go, and why

Three gates, three different jobs:

**Gate 1 — Plan approval (Stage 2, `SubagentStop`).** Implementation direction errors are expensive. A 30-second plan review is the cheapest correction in the pipeline. The hook blocks deterministically; it does not rely on Claude politely asking [5].

**Gate 2 — Merge approval (Stage 5, branch protection).** Enforced by GitHub infrastructure, not by Claude Code. This separation is the right architecture: the gate that should be hardest to bypass is the one furthest from the model.

**Gate 3 — Deploy confirmation (Stage 7, `PreToolUse`).** Deploy commands are irreversible on short timescales. The `PreToolUse` hook surfaces the exact command and requires explicit confirmation before anything hits production [5].

## Why subagents and not one big Skill

A single large Skill could describe the workflow as a checklist, but it would still run in one shared context. That means ticket intake, spec generation, implementation, tests, PR review, and deploy all compete for the same attention and memory budget.

Subagents turn those steps into isolated execution units. Each stage gets its own context window, its own system prompt, its own tool allowlist, and a structured handoff to the next stage [3]. That separation reduces context pollution, makes permission boundaries real instead of aspirational, and gives human approval points a clean place to stop the pipeline.

This is the deeper architectural reason the pipeline is composed from subagents rather than written as one giant instruction block. A Skill can explain the process; subagents enforce the separation that makes the process safe.

## Caveats and open questions

**Agent teams remain experimental.** As of the Week 16 changelog they require v2.1.32+ and are flagged experimental [4][7]. The shared task list, `TeammateIdle` and `TaskCompleted` hooks, plan-approval workflow — all of this can change. Do not build production infrastructure on the team coordination primitives yet.

**`/ultrareview` is metered.** Three free runs, then $5–$20 per run [8]. At $20/run with ten deploys a week, that is $200/week in security review costs. Budget this explicitly.

**The deploy stage is composition, not integration.** The pattern here is derivable from the hooks docs and is architecturally sound, but no published reference architecture documents the full Jira-to-deploy Claude Code pipeline end-to-end. Treat Stage 7 as a design pattern, not a documented integration.

**Atlassian OAuth scope management is your problem.** The MCP server handles the auth flow, but you still configure which Jira projects and issue types the integration can touch [1]. An overly permissive scope on a company-wide Jira is a real risk.

**`SubagentStop` fires for every subagent.** Selective gating requires conditional logic in the hook command or per-stage subagent definitions you can branch on [5]. There is no built-in matcher syntax for subagent names.

**`PostToolUse` cannot block.** If you use it for test feedback rather than a `SubagentStop` hook, a model that decides to ignore the failure can keep writing code. For hard enforcement, use `SubagentStop` with exit 2 [5].

**Ticket quality is the ceiling.** Vague acceptance criteria produce vague specs produce vague implementations. The pipeline amplifies ticket quality in both directions.
